content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Marisa Yeager 
3534 Beechwood Place 
Riverside, CA 92506 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-18-199 
Dear Ms. Yeager: 
October 16, 2018 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding gift provisions of the Political 
Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 _Please note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re 
Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and 
accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should 
contact us for additional advice. 
QUESTION 
Are payments for airfare, ground transportation, lodging, and meals made to you by the 
American Israel Education Foundation ("AIEF") to attend its upcoming "Educational Seminar in 
Israel for Southern Pacific Latino Leaders" (the "Seminar") subject to the Act's reporting 
requirements or its $470 annual gift limit for a gift or gifts from a single source in a calendar year? 
CONCLUSION 
Those payments would be reportable gifts under the Act, but would not be subject to the 
Act's $470 annual gift limit because the AIEF is a 501(c)(3) organization and because those 
payments are reasonably related to issues of national or international public policy. Any other 
payments from the AIEF to you, however, would be reportable gifts subject to the annual gift limit 
unless an exception applies. 
FACTS 
You are a Senior Manager of Intergovernmental Affairs and Transportation Policy for the 
Los Angeles County Metropolitan Transportation Authority, an appointee of the Governor to the 
46th District Agricultural Association, and the Political Director for Latinas Lead California, a 
nonpartisan organization "committed to electing Latina candidates who are passionate about 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-18-199 
Page No. 2 
creating effective public policies and fighting for reproductive rights, equal pay for equal work, 
economic prosperity, and a sustainable environment." 
The AIEF is a tax-exempt organization under Section 50l(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue 
Code affiliated with the American Israel Public Affairs Committee, a bipartisan organization of 
U.S. citizens committed to promoting the United States-Israel relationship in ways that enhance 
both countries' security. The AIEF has invited you to attend its upcoming Seminar from 
October 27, 2018, to November 4, 2018. According to the invitation, participants will include 
political and community leaders who seek to gain an in-depth understanding of modem Israel and 
the U.S.-Israel Relationship, and the Seminar will explore critical policy and security issues 
affecting both countries. The trip will also include a tour of religious sites in Jerusalem and the Sea 
of Galilee, and participants will take part in strategic surveys of Jerusalem and the Israeli borders 
with Lebanon, Syria, and Gaza. The AIEF will pay for your airfare, ground transportation, lodging, 
and meals associated with your participation in the Seminar. 
ANALYSIS 
The Act's Gift Provisions: 
Section 82028(a) defines a "gift" as: 
[ A]ny payment that confers a personal benefit on the recipient, to the extent 
that consideration of equal or greater value is not received and includes a 
rebate or discount in the price of anything of value unless the rebate or 
discount is made in the regular course of business to members of the public 
without regard to official status. 
In an effort to reduce improper influences on public officials, the Act regulates the receipt of 
gifts by an official in three ways: 
• 
Annual Gift Limit of $470: The Act prohibits certain officials from accepting a gift or gifts of 
more than $470 from a single source in a calendar year. (Section 89503 and Regulation 
18940.2.) 
' 
• 
Gift Reporting: The Act imposes reporting obligations on certain officials requiring that any 
gift of $50 or more ( or gifts that aggregate to $50 or more from the same source) received 
during the calendar year is disclosed on the official's statement of economic interests. (Section 
87207; see generally Sections 87200-87314.) 
• 
Conflict of Interest Disqualification: The Act prohibits an official from making, participating 
in making, or using his or her official position to influence the outcome of a governmental 
decision that would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the official's 
financial interest in the donor of a gift or gifts with an aggregate value of $4 70 or more provided 
to, received by, or promised to the official within 12 months prior to the date the decision is 
made. (Sections 87100 and 87103(e).) 


Gifts of Travel: Reporting 
File No. A-18-199 
Page No. 3 
Under the Act, travel is presumed to confer some personal benefit to an official. In addition, 
travel costs paid for by a third party generally are reportable gifts under the Act. Here, the AIEF has 
invited you to attend the Seminar in Israel and to pay for your airfare, ground transportation, 
lodging, and meals associated with the Seminar. Thus, these payments would be gifts from the 
AIEF to you, subject to reporting and regulation under the Act. (Section 82028; Williams Advice 
Letter, No. A-18-178; Hobbs Advice Letter, No. A-17-222.) 
Gifts of Travel: ~xceptions to the Annual Gift Limit 
Under some circumstances, even though a payment for transportation, lodging, and 
subsistence may be a reportable gift under the Act, it is not subject to the Act's current $470 annual 
gift limit. Section 89506(a)(2) sets forth an exception to the annual gift limit for certain travel 
payments, and provides in pertinent part as follows: 
(a) Payments, advances, or reimbursements for travel, including actual 
transportation and related lodging and subsistence that is reasonably related to 
a legislative or governmental purpose, or to an issue of state, national, or 
international public policy, are not prohibited or limited by this chapter if 
either of the following applies: 
(2) The travel is provided by a government, a governmental agency, a foreign 
government, a governmental authority, a bona fide public or private 
educational institution, as defined in Section 203 of the Revenue and Taxation 
Code, a nonprofit charitable or religious organization which is exempt from 
taxation under Section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code, or by a person 
domiciled outside the United States which substantially satisfies the 
requirements for tax-exempt status under Section 50l(c)(3) of the Internal 
Revenue Code. 
Thus, a travel payment to an official by a 501(c)(3) organization that is reasonably related to a 
legislative or governmental purpose, or to an issue of state, national, or international public policy, 
is not subject to the $470 annual gift limit of Section 89503. 
The AIEF is a 50l(c)(3) tax-exempt organization.2 Seminar participants will include 
political and community leaders who seek to gain an in-depth understanding of modem Israel and 
2 Please note that Regulation 18945(a) provides: "The person who makes the gift to the official(s) is the source 
of the gift unless that person is acting as an intermediary. The person is acting as an intermediary for the source of the 
gift when the gift to the official was provided under any of the following conditions: (1) the person receives a payment 
from a source and the payment is made to the official after the source identifies the official as the intended recipient of 
the gift; (2) the person receives a payment from a source after soliciting the payment with the understanding that the 
payment will be used for the sole or primary purpose of making a gift to an official; or (3) the person receives a 
payment from a source after the payment was solicited by the official or the official's agent for the purpose of making a 


File No. A-18-199 
Pugc No. 4 
the U.S.-Israel Relationship, and the Seminar will explore critical policy and security issues 
affecting both countries. Therefore, the travel associated with the Seminar is reasonably related to 
issues of national or international public policy. Thus, the AIEF's payments to you for airfare, 
ground transportation, lodging, and meals associated with the Seminar would not be subject to the 
Act's $470 annual gift limit pursuant to Section 89506(a)(2). However, any other payments from 
the AIEF to you such as expenses paid for entertainment purposes, would be reportable gifts subject 
to the $4 70 annual gift limit of Section 89503 unless an exception applies. 
Conflict of Interest Disqualification 
As noted above, the Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit an official from taking part 
in a governmental decision that would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the 
donor of a gift or gifts with an aggregate value of $470 or more to the official in the 12 months 
preceding the decision. (Sections 87100 and 87103(e).) Therefore, if the AIEF pays for the airfare, 
ground transportation, lodging, and meals associated with the Seminar, and those gifts exceed $470 
in value, the Act would prohibit you from taking part in a governmental decision that would have a 
disqualifying financial effect on the AIEF. 
Nonprofits that Regularly Host Travel for Elected Officials 
Finally, please be aware that Section 89506(f) provides that certain nonprofit organizations 
that regularly organize and host travel for elected officials must disclose certain donors. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
MFC:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
By: 
Matthew F. Christy 
Counsel, Legal Division 
gift to the official." The facts presented provide no indication that the AIEF is acting as an intermediary with respect to 
the payments at issue.

requirements or its $470 annual gift limit for a gift or gifts from a single source in a calendar year?
sections.conclusion:
Those payments would be reportable gifts under the Act, but would not be subject to the 
Act's $470 annual gift limit because the AIEF is a 501(c)(3) organization and because those 
payments are reasonably related to issues of national or international public policy. Any other

unless an exception applies.
